reasoning: Applied error taxonomy to 24 errors from lesson analysis. Selected 7 questions targeting the 4 highest-priority error patterns: third person -s (exercise_priority 5, 3 questions), preposition for time at/on (priority 5, 1 question), article omission (priority 4, 1 question), preposition for transport by (priority 4, 1 question), and present simple vs continuous (priority 4, 1 question). ZPD calibration based on A1+ assessment: zpd_lower (2 questions, 29%) for confidence building on recognized patterns, zpd_target (3 questions, 43%) at the learning boundary, zpd_stretch (2 questions, 29%) for production challenge. Used student's actual errors as distractors and Ukrainian L1 transfer patterns for plausible alternatives. Third person -s gets heaviest coverage as it is the core lesson target and most frequent error with emerging self-correction.
session_title: Practice: Daily routines and telling time - Dmytro (2026-03-21)

source_ref.original: My wife wake up at six because she go to school early
source_ref.corrected: My wife wakes up at six because she goes to school early

payload.title: Third person -s: daily routine verbs
payload.template_text: My wife {0} up at six o'clock because she {1} to school early.
payload.explanation: With he, she, it - add -s to the verb. 'She wakes up', 'she goes'. Verbs ending in -o add -es: go -> goes.
payload.content: Your original sentence: 'My wife wake up at six because she go to school early'

payload.title: Prepositions for time: at, on, in
payload.template_text: I start work {0} nine o'clock {1} Monday.
payload.explanation: Use 'at' for clock times (at nine o'clock), 'on' for days (on Monday), 'in' for months and seasons (in March, in winter).
payload.content: Your original sentence: 'I start work on nine o'clock'

payload.title: Which sentence uses the correct verb form?
payload.explanation: With third person singular subjects (he, she, it, the client), add -s to the verb: the client wants.
payload.content: Your original sentence: 'client want many things'

payload.title: Prepositions for transport
payload.template_text: I go to work {0} car. My wife goes {1} minibus.
payload.explanation: Use 'by' for mode of transport: by car, by bus, by train, by minibus. We say 'on foot' for walking.
payload.content: Your original sentence: 'I go on car'

payload.title: Third person -s: describe what she does
payload.template_text: She {0} amazing borshch. She {1} very well. She {2} books in the evening.
payload.explanation: Add -s for he/she/it: make -> makes, cook -> cooks, read -> reads. With verbs ending in consonant + e, just add -s.
payload.content: Describe your wife's routine using the correct verb forms.

source_ref.original: I am working on new project
source_ref.corrected: I am working on a new project

payload.title: 'I have lunch in office every day' is correct English
payload.explanation: We need articles: 'I have lunch in the office every day.' Use 'the' when both speakers know which office is meant.

source_ref.original: I am sometimes meeting with friends

payload.title: Sort: present simple (habits) or present continuous (right now)?

items.text: I drive to work every day.
I am driving to work right now.
She is cooking dinner at the moment.
I sometimes meet friends at the weekend.
I am meeting a friend today.

payload.explanation: Use present simple for habits and routines (every day, usually, always). Use present continuous for actions happening now or at this moment (right now, at the moment).
